reason: The item cleanly targets boundary-aware hazard assessment under reduced ambient pressure and rewards integrating multiple observables without collapsing to a single-variable safety claim. Decoys are largely single-fault and plausibly tempting (categorical safety from onset temperature; inferring energy dependence from mass-loss non-trend; equating no lift-off with negligible hazard), giving meaningful discrimination. Some options (especially E) are slightly more general/model-like than the observation set, and A’s “increasing operational risk” edges toward normative inference, but overall the admissible-vs-overreach boundaries remain clear.

The item has strong diagnostic intent around separating T2 vs T3 mechanisms and rejecting ISC-dominance and absolute-safety extrapolations, but some options blur mechanism vs model-error attribution, weakening boundary tightness and decoy single-fault purity. In particular, one incorrect option mixes multiple confusions, reducing clean discriminability and increasing the chance that expert disagreement stems from phrasing/attribution rather than the targeted thermal-runaway reasoning.

The item cleanly targets a specific mechanistic decoupling (triggering vs peak-temperature heat sources) and requires boundary-aware reasoning about why an intervention can shift T2 without materially lowering T3. The prompt is sufficiently constrained to the oxygen-pathway competition mechanism, discouraging common misattributions (e.g., conflating onset control with total heat suppression) while still providing strong expert-level discrimination. Minor ambiguity remains because it is short-answer with no decoys, so scoring consistency depends on rubric strictness rather than option design.
